intro: 2007. You run Research In Motion. The BlackBerry is the phone of presidents and CEOs — the keyboard and secure email are so addictive people call it 'CrackBerry.' Apple just revealed a keyboard-less touchscreen phone. Business will NEVER trust a toy, you tell yourself, sweating slightly.

realOutcome.valuation: phone business effectively worth zero by 2016
realOutcome.summary: BlackBerry went from defining the smartphone to quitting phones entirely. Every 'what actually happened' below is real. Brutal fall.

decisions.scenario: The iPhone drops. No physical keyboard, weak enterprise security, dogwater battery. Your leadership calls it a consumer toy that serious professionals will laugh at.

choices.text: Dismiss it. Business needs a keyboard and security.
choices.reveal: RIM's co-CEOs genuinely thought touchscreens were a fad and keyboards + battery life would keep them on top. They were confidently describing their own museum exhibit. Oof.

choices.text: Panic-build a real touchscreen BlackBerry now
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take the iPhone seriously in 2007 and pair a modern touchscreen with the security enterprises trust. BlackBerry rides the wave UP instead of getting dragged under.

choices.text: Open BBM to every platform while it's still beloved
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: BBM was the messaging app EVERYONE wanted. Freed from BlackBerry hardware in 2007, it's basically WhatsApp before WhatsApp. Generational fumble avoided.

decisions.scenario: The pressure to answer the iPhone is insane. You can rush a touchscreen phone — the Storm — out THIS year before it's ready, or take the time to build it properly.

choices.text: Rush the Storm out to clap back at the iPhone fast
choices.reveal: The Storm shipped in 2008 with a cursed clicking screen and buggy software. Carriers reportedly returned them in bulk. The rushed clapback hurt the brand worse than saying nothing. Self-report.

choices.text: Take the time to build the touchscreen right
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A polished first touchscreen — even a year late — protects your reputation for quality instead of nuking it on a half-baked gimmick.

choices.text: Skip touch, go all-in on keyboards + great apps
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You own the keyboard niche unapologetically and actually fund a real app store. A smaller but defensible BlackBerry survives with the loyalists.

decisions.scenario: Developers are stampeding to Apple's App Store and Android. Your platform is miserable to build for. Invest heavily in apps and dev tools, or protect the crown jewels — email and BBM?

choices.text: Protect email + BBM, neglect the app ecosystem
choices.reveal: RIM under-invested in apps, betting email and messaging would carry it. But a smartphone with no apps became unsellable and the gap turned into the Grand Canyon. Cope didn't scale.

choices.text: Invest hard in apps and elite dev tools
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give developers a reason to build for BlackBerry. Apps + your security + the keyboard keep enterprise and power users locked in.

choices.text: Adopt Android to inherit its whole app library
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You slap the world's apps onto hardware people already trust for security and typing. BlackBerry becomes THE premium enterprise Android phone.

decisions.scenario: You're launching the PlayBook tablet to fight the iPad. Small issue: at launch it won't have a native email app — on a device from the company that literally invented mobile email.

choices.text: Ship the PlayBook now, no native email
choices.reveal: The PlayBook launched in 2011 with NO native email client — from the email company. It flopped and RIM took a giant writedown on unsold stock. You had one job.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hold the launch until the tablet does the ONE thing your brand is famous for. A finished product protects the reputation a broken one torches.

choices.text: Cancel the tablet, focus everything on phones
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You refuse to fight Apple on tablets and dump the money into fixing your phone platform, where you still have a puncher's chance.

decisions.scenario: It's late. Bet everything on a brand-new OS, BlackBerry 10, arriving YEARS after iOS and Android locked the market — or pivot toward what the company might actually survive as.

choices.reveal: BB10 launched in 2013 to a market that had fully moved on. Sales were grim, RIM ate a $4.4B loss, and it bailed on consumer phones soon after. Showed up to the party after everyone left.

choices.text: Pivot early to secure software and services
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You accept the phone is cooked and become the security/enterprise software company BlackBerry eventually (way too late) became. Doing it in 2013 saves years and billions.

choices.text: Just sell the company at its 2008 peak instead
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You read the room while you're still worth $80B and hand shareholders a massive exit at the top. Selling the top > fighting the tide. Diamond hands are not always the move.

bands.line: You saw the touchscreen coming and adapted. Villain episode: WON. No notes.
A smaller, focused survivor. Not the king, but not a ghost. Mid.
The keyboard company the touchscreen erased. Faithful to history. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Apple erased BlackBerry's lead in five years. Today's agents flagged a market leader calling its disruptor a toy →
